observables: SCATTERED SPIDER logs into Okta accounts across multiple victim companies almost certainly using the same device
SCATTERED SPIDER primarily focused on capturing credentials and using these credentials to authenticate to VPN, Citrix or Okta portals.
Likely exploitation of OpenAM vulnerability CVE-2021-35464
In multiple cases, the adversary attempted to leverage access from initial victims to access cellular providers.
Called the helpdesk to reset the victim’s authentication factor
Employed voice phishing against the victim’s helpdesk to obtain access to an Entra ID identity
Phishing emails and SMS messages directed users to credential harvesting pages
SCATTERED SPIDER setup SSO phishing pages
Limited use of Impacket’s WMIExec.py tool to move laterally
Used schtasks.exe utility to run UAC-bypass binary
Used Azure virtual machine run a PowerShell script that installed AAD Internals and setup a backdoor using ConvertTo-AADIntBackdoor method
Used PS to attempt to download various RMM tools and ran numerous PS scripts to gather information

Used PS to attempt to download various RMM tools and ran numerous PS scripts to gather information.

Uses the AADInternals PowerShell module to interact with Microsoft Entra ID.

Uses PowerShell to reboot Windows systems into safe mode: powershell.exe -WindowStyle Hidden -Command "bcdedit /set '{default}' safeboot network; shutdown /r"
Used command shell to run numerous system utilities
The Kohgapy Python executes commands like ls and chmod +x in a Unix Shell on the target host
Installed Python to execute Impacket’s Python utilities

Installed Python to execute Impacket’s Python utilities.

Uses the Kohgapy Python script to Deploy ransomware to ESXi Hosts
In some cases, users were socially engineered via phone to navigate to a malicious link to download malware
Began services on remote hosts using PSExec or RemCom
Created and executed Azure Automation jobs through Azure Portal (Write an Azure Automation runbook, Create an Azure Automation job)

Captured accounts allowed the adversary to maintain access to most victim environments without malware. To maintain persistent access, the adversary continued to log in with valid credentials when possible.
Accessed several Entra ID accounts
Controlled Okta users and Entra ID users
Used net to change several existing users’ passwords and modified UAC restrictions on certain passwords. On AWS, the adversary modified the existing role to escalate user privileges.
Added AWS IAM access keys to pre-existing users (CreateAccessKey)
Added credentials to Entra ID application registration (corresponding Entra ID audit log event: Update application – Certificates and secrets management )
Created a new access key for an AWS account (CreateAccessKey)
Reset passwords of Okta users (user.mfa.factor.deactivate). Added backdoor access key (CreateAccessKey) and console login password for backdoor AWS IAM user (CreateLoginProfile)
SCATTERED SPIDER has created an access key for a pre-existing IAM user (CreateAccessKey) and attempted to create AWS login profile user password (UpdateLoginProfile)
Created a new SSH key for an adversary-created EC2 instance (CreateKeyPair)
Registered a phone app as an additional authentication factor in Entra ID (Update user)
To circumvent MFA, the actor registered adversary-controlled devices with Azure AD/Microsoft Authenticator or Google Authenticator.
SCATTERED SPIDER in some cases created additional domain accounts to enable persistent access
Created backdoor Entra ID (Add user) and AWS IAM user (CreateUser)

Registered the Afi Backup Entra ID app which lead to the creation of a service principal (corresponding Entra ID audit log event: Add service principal) and registered the Veeam backup application in Entra ID (corresponding Entra ID audit log event: Add application)
Activated Cross-tenant access via Entra ID Cross-Tenant Synchronization
SCATTERED SPIDER added an additional actor-controlled identity provider to Okta and added a federated domain to Azure AD
Used Invoke-ACLPwn to attempt to elevate privileges
Ran likely UAC-bypass binaries or disabled UAC restrictions by modifying accounts
SCATTERED SPIDER has used the publicly available bdvl rootkit on ESXi systems
To evade static AV scanning, the adversary used Pumpy to increase the size of binaries on disk.
Used the uncommon net1 utility, likely to avoid net-related detections

technique_name: Match Legitimate Resource Name or Location
observables: Named newly provisioned VMs according to victim VM-naming scheme
SCATTERED SPIDER has used the following command to display and clear event logs: wevtutil el | Foreach-Object {wevtutil cl $_}
Deleted emails that were triggered by the adversaries activity e.g., emails related to the enrollment and resetting of MFA (MoveToDeletedItems)
Removed emails connected to the password reset events (HardDelete)
Added two actor-controlled federated domains in Entra ID (Add unverified domain, Verify domain, Add verified domain, Set domain authentication)
Converted preexisting managed Entra ID domain to federated to add a federated identity provider (Set domain authentication)
In one case, SCATTERED SPIDER authenticated via an allowlisted IP address, then transferred a session cookie to enable a new session from a different IP address
On AWS, the adversary disabled one user’s MFA. Separately, the adversary attempted to disable MFA enforcement for several on-premises users.
Reset MFA for Okta users via socially engineering the helpdesk
Attempted to deactivate GuardDuty (DeleteDetector) and CloudWatch alarms (DeleteAlarms)

SCATTERED SPIDER often uses the publicly available script privacy-script.bat, which contains commands to disable Microsoft Defender functionality.

ESXi execInstalledOnly security control disabled.

To disable or uninstall EDR, SCATTERED SPIDER used regedit, batch scripts, or binaries to modify select registry values. After making these modifications, the adversary attempted to reboot victim machines in safe mode. The adversary also attempted to disable Duo enforcement via registry key modifications.
Unset the HISTFILE variable to prevent command logging on Linux hosts in AWS
Altered firewall rules on AWS to allow ingress from a specific AWS subnet

Altered firewall rules on AWS to allow ingress from a specific AWS subnet.

SCATTERED SPIDER often uses the publicly available script privacy-script.bat which contains commands to modify firewall settings
Deleted VPC firewall rules and added a new one that allowed egress traffic to all IP addresses (DeleteNetworkAclEntry, CreateNetworkAclEntry)
Modified security groups (AuthorizeSecurityGroupIngress) and AWS Firewall (DeleteNetworkAclEntry, CreateNetworkAclEntry, ReplaceNetworkAclEntry) to publicly expose an RDS instance
Sets firewall rules for actor-created EC2 instances to allow all incoming RDP or all traffic
Attempted to update CloudTrail trail settings (UpdateTrail) and to delete flow logs (DeleteFlowLogs)
SCATTERED SPIDER reboots Windows systems into safe mode using the command powershell.exe -WindowStyle Hidden -Command "bcdedit /set '{default}' safeboot network; shutdown /r"
Created EC2 instances in victim's AWS environment to mount the disk and dump the Active Directory credentials of the domain controller running on a preexisting EC2 instance in the same environment
Created VMs in Azure (Create or Update Virtual Machine) and AWS (RunInstances)
Provisioned new VMs within Azure to bypass VPN authentication while accessing on-premises network
Reset password for Azure VM.
Mimikatz and MAGNET Ram Capture can harvest LSASS credentials.
Dumped password hashes from NTDS using Gosecretsdump
The adversary used secretsdump.py and Volume Shadow Copy (with wmic shadowcopy create) to access NTDS.DIT.
The adversary used secretsdump.py, GoSecretsDump, and Volume Shadow Copy (with wmic shadowcopy create) to access NTDS.DIT.
SCATTERED SPIDER ran DCSync to pull passwords from victim domain controllers.
Phishing pages captured credentials users willingly provided
SCATTERED SPIDER has installed the Cookie Quick Manager and EditThisCookie browser extensions to export session cookies

In one case, the adversary obtained a VPN-authentication certificate from a victim server. Separately, on AWS, the adversary used cat and grep to identify credentials in files on S3. To attempt to capture unsecured credentials, the adversary queried the AWS-instance metadata API at two different victims.

Retrieved credentials for Azure Storage (corresponding Azure activity log event: List Storage Account Keys); Almost certainly used MicroBurst to discover credentials accessible to Automation Accounts in Azure (corresponding Azure activity log event: Create or Update an Azure Automation Runbook)
Copied data.mdb file containing the vCenter SAML private signing key
In one case, the adversary obtained a VPN-authentication certificate from a victim server. Separately, on AWS, the adversary used cat and grep to identify credentials in files on S3.
To attempt to capture unsecured credentials, the adversary queried the AWS-instance metadata API at two different victims.
Downloaded credentials from privileged access management solution
Used actor-controlled Okta users to access privileged access management system to obtain Entra ID account credentials.
Listed secrets in AWS Secrets Manager (ListSecrets)
Almost certainly attempted to obtain Entra Connect Sync credentials
Almost certainly attempted to forge a SAML token for vCenter Single-Sign-On
SCATTERED SPIDER typically attempted to authenticate using usernames and passwords, leading to generation of MFA push notifications to fatigue users
The adversary heavily used ping to test connectivity to file-sharing sites and RMM tool-hosting sites.

To employ System Network Configuration Discovery, a threat actor attempts to collect details about a network configuration and its settings. Multiple tools are available to help actors leverage this technique, including arp, ipconfig/ifconfig, ping, nltest, and route.

Threat actors commonly use multiple discovery techniques immediately following initial access and frequently employ additional discovery techniques during intrusions using LOLbins, including Security Software Discovery (T1018.001), Domain Trust Discovery (T1482) using nltest, System Information Discovery (T1082), System Owner/User Discovery (T1033), and Account Discovery (T1087).

SCATTERED SPIDER extensively uses the ping utility to test connectivity to various legitimate file- or RMM-hosting sites. In early 2023, the adversary accessed multiple North America-based technology companies that specialize in Business Process Outsourcing (BPO) and used the ping command to test connectivity to two different North America-based cellular providers.
Additionally, SCATTERED SPIDER used LOTL utilities, including ping and net, to discover information about network architecture and Active Directory (AD) information. SCATTERED SPIDER has employed this technique on both on-premise and Cloud environments.
The adversary heavily used ping to test connectivity to file-sharing sites and RMM tool-hosting sites.
The adversary used Advanced Port Scanner to enable internal reconnaissance.
SCATTERED SPIDER used ADRecon to gather information about domain accounts, trusts, group policy objects, and permission groups.

Listed AWS IAM groups (ListGroups), roles (ListRoles), and policies (ListPolicies, ListGroupsForUser)
Downloaded Entra ID role assignments (Download role assignments - finished (bulk))
The adversary manually searches for and downloads victim data
SCATTERED SPIDER used ADRecon and ADExplorer to gather information about domain accounts, trusts, group policy objects, and permission groups.
SCATTERED SPIDER used ADRecon to gather information about domain accounts, trusts, group policy objects, and permission groups.
Listed AWS IAM users (ListUsers)
SCATTERED SPIDER used ScoutSuite to discover information about AWS accounts.
SCATTERED SPIDER used ADRecon to gather information about domain accounts, trusts, group policy objects, and permission groups.
SCATTERED SPIDER used ScoutSuite and aws ssm describe-instance-information to discover information about AWS services running in the victim environment.
Used MyApps to traverse to Entra ID SSO Applications
Looked at EC2 management dashboard to view EC2 instances
Listed EC2 instances (DescribeInstances) and RDS databases (DescribeDBInstances, DescribeDBSnapshots)
SCATTERED SPIDER used AWS IMDS to gather information about IP addresses and subnets within the victim environment.
SCATTERED SPIDER used ADRecon to gather information about domain accounts, trusts, group policy objects, and permission groups.
Listed S3 buckets and objects (ListBuckets, ListObjects)
SCATTERED SPIDER enumerated S3 buckets using the S3Browser utility.
The adversary used mstsc.exe to establish RDP connections within victim environments.
In one case, SCATTERED SPIDER attempted to deploy RemCom using Impacket’s smbexec.py tool.
Moved laterally via SSH to vCenter server appliance
Particularly for Linux environments, the adversary used SSH keys to move laterally.
SSH used for lateral movement to ESXi servers
In limited circumstances, the adversary used WinRM to run commands on remote services.
Used Okta SSO to move laterally to AWS
SCATTERED SPIDER opened Citrix configuration files using text editors such as Notepad++.
Enabled anonymous access to OneDrive (SharingPolicyChanged)
Accessed emails using added Entra ID enterprise apps (MailItemsAccessed)
SCATTERED SPIDER leverages email-forwarding rules for defense evasion, typically to prevent users from receiving suspicious account activity notifications or security alerts
Collected RDS database snapshot via export feature (StartExportTask)
The adversary attempted to capture data from internal customer/user databases, particularly CRM applications
Accessed wikipages
Downloaded data from SharePoint in particular security relevant files related to MFA and VPN setup
From victim SharePoint repositories, SCATTERED SPIDER gathered information on networking, MFA and remote access, and user onboarding.
Downloaded data from S3 buckets
SCATTERED SPIDER gathered data from S3 buckets; in a separate case, the adversary accessed corporate information on GSuite.
SCATTERED SPIDER has attempted to send victim data from compromised email addresses to adversary-owned email addresses

Downloaded data directly from SharePoint (FileDownloaded).
Downloaded objects from S3 bucket including parts of the exported database snapshot via the AWS Console (GetObject)
Downloaded data from AWS S3 buckets using S3 Browser
SCATTERED SPIDER has attempted to exfiltrate data to adversary-controlled GitHub repositories
Exfiltrated data from Snowflake to AWS S3 bucket
SCATTERED SPIDER exfiltrated some Jenkins-related files from AWS to transfer.sh and exfiltrated a file from a Docker container to bashupload[.]com.
While most incidents did not involve malware, in one case, the adversary deployed several legitimate RMM tools and malicious RATs, which use web protocols for C2
SCATTERED SPIDER used a Twingate connector as an internal proxy to obtain and maintain network access to private resources
SCATTERED SPIDER deployed several proxy tools, including rsocx.
SCATTERED SPIDER often downloads tooling from filebin[.]net, transfer[.]sh, and upload[.]ee, as well as remote S3 buckets, BackBlaze, and shz[.]al URLs
SCATTERED SPIDER employed an unprecedented variety of legitimate RMM tools to maintain persistent access.
Some remote administration tools used by SCATTERED SPIDER used high TCP ports for C2 (4444, 5565, and 29381)
Deleted AMIs (DeregisterImage) and EC2 instance snapshots (DeleteSnapshot); deleted and deactivated AWS Backups (DeleteRecoveryPoint)
Deployed AlphV ransomware to ESXi systems, Azure, and AWS VMs
SCATTERED SPIDER has used Qilin ransomware and ALPHA SPIDER's Alphv ransomware to encrypt data on both Windows and ESXi systems.
One of SCATTERED SPIDER’s EDR-bypass tools attempts to reboot a system in safe mode. Additionally, the adversary attempted to reboot an AWS host when troubleshooting.
One of SCATTERED SPIDER’s EDR-bypass tools attempts to reboot a system in safe mode. Additionally, the adversary has attempted to reboot an AWS host when troubleshooting.

Shutdown EC2 instance hosting domain controller to detach the mounted disks. Then mounted the disks in an actor-created EC2 instance to dump the NTDS.dit. Ultimately reattached the disks back to the original EC2 instance hosting the domain controller and rebooted the instance.
On AWS, SCATTERED SPIDER deleted one user’s access key.
Reset the authentication factor to obtain access to the Entra ID account; access removal was not the primary objective

SCATTERED SPIDER often uses VMware PowerCLI to change root account passwords on ESXi hypervisors to complicate recovery.

On AWS, SCATTERED SPIDER deleted one user’s access key.
SCATTERED SPIDER set up numerous phishing domains to harvest credentials
